title: Safe downcast with dynamic_cast
prompt_md:
## Goal

Objects arrive one at a time, each handed to you only as a `Base*`, even though some of them are actually `Derived` objects. A `Derived` has an extra capability that `Base` lacks: a member function `special()` that returns the string `I am Derived`. Working purely through the `Base*`, decide at runtime whether each object really is a `Derived`, and if so invoke `special()` on it.

Design `Base` and `Derived` so that this runtime check is even possible (recall what `dynamic_cast` requires of a type), then perform the check yourself.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 10) -- the number of objects.
- Next `N` lines: each is either `base` or `derived`, the kind of object to create (always held in a `Base*`).

## Output

For each object, print one line:
- If it is genuinely a `Derived`: `Cast succeeded: I am Derived`
- Otherwise: `Cast failed: base object`

test_cases.label: Mixed base and derived
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Cast failed: base object
Cast succeeded: I am Derived
Cast failed: base object

test_cases.label: All derived
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Cast succeeded: I am Derived
Cast succeeded: I am Derived

test_cases.label: Longer interleaved run
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Cast succeeded: I am Derived
Cast failed: base object
Cast failed: base object
Cast succeeded: I am Derived
Cast failed: base object

title: Counting derived types in a polymorphic collection
prompt_md:
## Goal

You maintain a collection of animals stored polymorphically as `Animal*`. Three concrete kinds exist: `Dog`, `Cat`, and `Puppy` -- and **a `Puppy` is a kind of `Dog`** (`Puppy` publicly derives from `Dog`). A `Dog` can `bark()` and a `Cat` can `meow()`; a `Puppy`, being a `Dog`, also barks. Given a target *category* to search for, walk the collection and, using a runtime-checked downcast, invoke the category's behavior on exactly the objects that really belong to it, counting how many you find.

The catch: the input word that created an object is **not** its category. Searching for `dog` must match every object that is a `Dog` at runtime -- including every `Puppy`, since a puppy is-a dog. The literal token `puppy` is not the word `dog`, so a plain string comparison against the input word gives the wrong answer; only a `dynamic_cast<Dog*>` correctly recognizes a puppy as a dog.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 20) -- the number of animals.
- Next `N` lines: one of `dog <name>`, `cat <name>`, or `puppy <name>` (`<name>` is a single word).
- Final line: the search category, either `dog` or `cat`.

## Output

For each animal that belongs to the search category at runtime, in collection order, print:
- a `Dog` or `Puppy` named `<name>`: `Dog: <name> says Woof` (this is what `Dog::bark()` returns; a puppy inherits it)
- a `Cat` named `<name>`: `Cat: <name> says Meow` (this is what `Cat::meow()` returns)

Then print `Found: <count>`, the number of matches. If none match, print only `Found: 0`.

test_cases.label: Find dogs -- puppies count as dogs
test_cases.stdin:
5
dog Rex
cat Whiskers
puppy Buddy
dog Max
cat Luna
dog
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Dog: Rex says Woof
Dog: Buddy says Woof
Dog: Max says Woof
Found: 3

test_cases.label: Find cats when only dogs and puppies exist
test_cases.stdin:
3
puppy Spot
dog Zeus
puppy Rex
cat

test_cases.label: Find dogs in an all-puppy collection
test_cases.stdin:
3
puppy Coco
puppy Bella
puppy Toby
dog
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Dog: Coco says Woof
Dog: Bella says Woof
Dog: Toby says Woof
Found: 3

test_cases.label: Find cats in a mix of cats, dogs, and puppies
test_cases.stdin:
5
cat Mittens
puppy Bruno
cat Oreo
dog Zeus
puppy Tom
cat
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Cat: Mittens says Meow
Cat: Oreo says Meow
Found: 2
